---
title: "Data, Evidence, and Decision Making (with R)"
author: "Ayush Patel"
subtitle: "Session 2"
institute: "National Law School of India University, Bengaluru"
date: today
date-format: "DD MMM, YYYY"
embed-resources: true
bibliography: citations.bib
execute: 
  echo: false
format: 
  revealjs:
    fig-height: 6
    fig-width: 14
    embed-resources: true
    margin-left: 50px
    margin-right: 50px
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1400
    height: 850
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    footer: "Email: ayush.ap58@gmail.com"
---

# Readings 

It is expected that you have completed the following readings:

1. Chapter 1: Hello Data of @cetinkaya-rundel2024ims2
2. Chapter 1: Getting Things in Proportion of @spiegelhalter2019art
3. Chapter 2: Summarising and Communicating Numbers of @spiegelhalter2019art

# Understanding Data

## Data

> Information gathered through various sources or methods that is structured in a manner that facilitates analyses is called data.

<br>

*Though data can take various forms, in this class we will focus on data that can be structured in a table.*

## Data

<br>

1. *Who/What* is your data about? Humans, cars, students, penguins, flights?
2. Which *details* are recorded? income, speed, grades, species, delay?

> The `Who/What` is the **Unit of observations** or rows and the `details` are **variables** or columns.

## You have met the penguins before {.smaller}


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}


```{r}
#| label: display-penguins

library(palmerpenguins)

penguins
```


:::

::: {.column width="40%"} 

1. `Who/What`: Each row is a unique adult penguin. This is the *unit of observation*.
2. `Details`: For each penguins, it's bill length, bill depth, flipper length, body mass, sex, species, island, and year of record are recorded. These are the *Variables*.
3. The total number of rows are called *Observations*.
4. This data has 344 Observations and 8 variables.

:::

::::

## You try

```{r}
#| label: display-

openintro::starbucks
```

1. What is the unit of observation? How many total observations?
2. What do you think are the units of the variable?

## Tidy Data

@tidy-data provides a definitions and reference of what should tidy data look like. Data presented in tidy format is more convenient to use for analysis in most cases. Art by @horst2026tidydata explains the idea.

![Tidy Data](https://data.europa.eu/apps/data-visualisation-guide/Tidy%20data%208b1465b3f83a442f97d1d33ce400dbe7/tidydata_1.jpg)


# Variable Types

> Variables can be used to record different kinds of information. Weight(number), Dead(Binary), ice cream flavour (categorical). It is important to make these distinction so as to carry out appropriate analysis.

## Numerical

<br><br>

What is the difference between :

1. Amount of rice consumed in a restaurant 
2. The number of customers that visited the restaurant

## Numerical

![[Allison Horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else)](https://cdn.myportfolio.com/45214904-6a61-4e23-98d6-b140f8654a40/5c0b4bea-b51d-42cb-b0bb-1bc1d59ac0c5_rw_1920.png?h=21e2134daab3967c99eb572c70c77f56)

## Categorical

<br><br>

What is the difference between :

1. Did they have to wait at the restaurant? 
2. What are the dishes that they ordered?
3. How did they like the food? (yuck, bad, ok ok, good, great)

## Categorical

![[Allison Horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else)](https://cdn.myportfolio.com/45214904-6a61-4e23-98d6-b140f8654a40/38f5ed46-9dcd-4981-9ae0-497fb20aa65c_rw_1920.png?h=9c0c15395b2471d111aa26974d8abede)

# Looking at data

<br><br>

> Nothing builds intuition better than looking at data. Nothing makes you wonder more than looking at data. *When done right*

## Babies {.smaller}


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

```{r}
#| label: show-babies

library(openintro)
library(tidyverse)
library(hrbrthemes)

babies |>
  glimpse()

```


:::

::: {.column}

|Variable|Definition|
|--------|----------|
|case|id number|
|bwt|birthweight, in ounces|
|gestation|length of gestation, in days|
|parity|binary indicator for a first pregnancy (0 = first pregnancy)|
|age|mother's age in years|
|height|mother's height in inches|
|weight|mother's weight in pounds|
|smoke|binary indicator for whether the mother smokes|

Source: Child Health and Development Studies


:::

::::

## Baby weights{.nostretch}

```{r}
#| label: fig-baby-wts-1
#| fig-cap: A histogram of baby weight in ounces
#| fig-dpi: 500

babies |>
  ggplot(aes(bwt))+
  geom_histogram(colour = "#ffffff") +
  labs(
    title = "Baby Weights at birth",
    x = "Birth Weight in Ounces",
    y = "Number of babies"
  )+
  theme_ipsum_es()
```


## Baby weights{.nostretch}

```{r}
#| label: fig-baby-wts-2
#| fig-cap: A boxplot of baby weight in ounces
#| fig-dpi: 500

babies |>
  ggplot(aes("A",bwt))+
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Baby Weights at birth",
    y = "Birth Weight in Ounces",
    x = NULL
  )+
  theme_ipsum_es(grid = "X") +
  coord_flip()+
  theme(axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y =  element_blank()  
  )
```

## First Pregnancy

<br><br>

```{r}
#| label: tbl-first-pregnancy
#| tbl-cap: Share of women that experienced their fitst pregnancy

babies |>
  janitor::tabyl(parity) |>
  janitor::adorn_totals("row")|>
  janitor::adorn_pct_formatting() |>
  rename(`Fisrt Pregnancy` = parity) |>
  kableExtra::kable()
```

## First Pregnancy{.nostretch}

<br>

```{r}
#| label: fig-first-preg
#| fig-cap: Share of women that experienced their first pregnancy
#| fig-dpi: 500

babies |>
  ggplot(aes("A")) +
  geom_bar(aes(fill = as.factor(parity)), position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_ft(name = "Pregnancy",label = c("Not fisrt","First")) +
  scale_y_percent()+
  labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "Proportion of women",
    x = NULL
  ) +
    theme_ipsum_es(grid = "X") + 
    theme(
      legend.direction = "horizontal",
      legend.position = "top",
      axis.title.y = element_blank(),
      axis.text.y =  element_blank(),
      legend.title.position = "top"
      )+
    coord_flip()
```

# Proportions

<br><br>

> Comparisons and Communication

## 

# Summary Statistics

> A summary statistic is a number that conveys some key feature of a bunch of numbers.

## Why do we need summary statistics

<br>

Number of times I crave for Biryani every week. Data for the past 50 weeks:

```{r}
#| label: why-summary-stat

round(rnorm(50,10,3))

```

What can you say about my wish to consume Biryani?

## Why do we need summary statistics

Animals I spotted on the road in the last 50 days:

```{r}
#| label: spot-animals

sample(c("Cat","Dog","Cow","Turtle","Snake","Eagle","Mouse","Rabbit"),80,T)

```

How would you summarise the animals I saw in the past 50 days?

# References
